title: Agent Handoff
tldr: Pattern where one agent passes control and accumulated context to another agent better suited for the next sub-task.
plain_english: Imagine a customer-support agent that handles general questions but transfers you to billing when you mention an invoice. Agent handoff is the same idea inside an AI system: one agent realises a different agent is better equipped to continue, packages up what it learned, and hands off the conversation. The receiving agent picks up with full context instead of starting over.
how_it_works: The active agent decides a handoff is needed (via a routing rule, a tool call, or its own LLM judgment), serializes the relevant state — user goal, conversation summary, intermediate results, open tool calls — and invokes the target agent. The target agent receives that bundle as its starting context and continues the loop. Some frameworks (OpenAI Swarm, LangGraph) model handoff as a special tool the LLM can call by name; others use explicit graph edges. The shared state lives in a session store so it survives the transition.
why_it_matters: Handoff lets you specialise. Instead of a single mega-agent that knows everything, you get small, well-tested agents (router, billing, refunds, escalation) that hand work between each other. This keeps prompts focused, reduces tool-set bloat, and makes failures easier to diagnose because each agent has a narrow remit.
example: A travel-booking system uses three agents: triage (intent), flights (search + book), and changes (modify existing trips). When the user says 'I need to change my Tuesday flight,' triage detects the intent, calls handoff to changes, and passes {user_id, booking_ref, change_request}. Changes resumes with that state and never asks the user to repeat themselves.
pitfalls: State leakage: handing off too much context bloats the next agent's prompt and inflates token cost; pass only what the target needs.
Loops: agent A hands to B, B hands back to A, repeat; add a handoff-depth counter and a hard cap to break cycles.
Lost intent: serialising state via JSON drops nuance the LLM picked up; include a short natural-language summary alongside structured fields.
Auth and permissions: a handoff target may not have the same tool/data access as the source; validate scopes before passing control.
when_use: Use handoff when responsibilities cleanly split by domain (billing vs support), when one agent's tools shouldn't be exposed to the other, or when a senior model takes over from a cheaper triage model.
when_avoid: Avoid for simple linear chains where every step is mandatory — that's a pipeline, not a handoff. Also avoid when latency budget is tight; each handoff adds a cold-start LLM call.

faq.q: How is agent handoff different from a tool call?
faq.a: A tool call returns a result back to the calling agent which keeps control. A handoff transfers control: the new agent owns the next turn and decides what to do, including handing back if needed.

faq.q: Do I need shared memory for handoffs?
faq.a: Yes — without shared session state the receiving agent starts blank and the user repeats themselves. A simple key-value store keyed on conversation_id is enough for most setups.

faq.q: Can two agents hand off to each other?
faq.a: They can, but track handoff depth and require a non-trivial reason for each transition. A common bug is two agents ping-ponging because each thinks the other is better suited.

faq.q: Is handoff the same as routing?
faq.a: Routing usually means picking which agent runs first. Handoff happens mid-conversation when the active agent decides someone else should take over. Routing is one-time, handoff is dynamic.

title: Agent Orchestrator
tldr: Coordinator that decides which agent runs next, manages shared state, and enforces global rules across a multi-agent system.
plain_english: Think of an orchestrator as the project manager for a team of agents. Each agent does one thing well — research, code, write — but somebody has to decide who works on what, in what order, and what the final answer is. The orchestrator owns that. It reads the user's goal, picks an agent, feeds it context, takes back the result, decides the next step, and stops when the goal is met.
how_it_works: The orchestrator holds a plan (or builds one with an LLM call), maintains a shared blackboard of intermediate results, and dispatches work to specialist agents one at a time or in parallel. After each agent completes, the orchestrator updates the plan, decides the next agent, and re-dispatches. Stopping conditions live in the orchestrator: max steps, budget, terminal state, or explicit user-confirmed answer. Frameworks like CrewAI and AutoGen ship with orchestrator classes; LangGraph models orchestration as a graph the orchestrator traverses.
why_it_matters: Without an orchestrator, every agent has to know about every other agent — coupling explodes. With one, you keep agents simple and put coordination in one place where it's easy to test, log, and change. It also gives you a single point to enforce policies (cost cap, tool budget, escalation to humans) instead of scattering them across N agents.
example: A research-report orchestrator receives 'Write a 2-page brief on small language models.' It plans: outline → research-agent gathers sources → analysis-agent extracts claims → writer-agent drafts sections → editor-agent reviews. The orchestrator dispatches each step, stores intermediate results on the blackboard, and stops when the editor approves.
pitfalls: Single point of failure: if the orchestrator crashes mid-run, all agent state is lost; persist the blackboard between steps.
Tight coupling to LLM judgment: an LLM-based orchestrator that decides next steps token-by-token can be expensive and inconsistent; cache plans where possible.
Plan staleness: if early steps invalidate the original plan, the orchestrator must re-plan or it'll dispatch agents toward an outdated goal.
Hidden costs: each orchestrator turn is its own LLM call; track tokens spent on coordination separately from work done by specialist agents.
when_use: Use when you have three or more agents, need centralised policy enforcement, or want to swap agents in and out without changing how the system as a whole behaves.
when_avoid: Avoid for two-agent systems where direct handoff is simpler, or when latency demands a single LLM call — orchestrators add at least one extra hop per turn.

faq.q: Should the orchestrator itself be an LLM?
faq.a: Sometimes. A deterministic orchestrator (state machine) is cheaper and more predictable; an LLM orchestrator handles open-ended workflows better. Many production systems use a hybrid: LLM picks the next agent, code enforces stopping rules.

faq.q: How does an orchestrator differ from a router?
faq.a: A router picks one agent and sends the request once. An orchestrator runs a multi-step loop, calling several agents in sequence or parallel and combining their results.

faq.q: What state should the orchestrator track?
faq.a: User goal, current plan, completed steps, intermediate results (the blackboard), error log, and budget consumed. Keep it serialisable so you can resume after a crash.

faq.q: Can orchestrators run agents in parallel?
faq.a: Yes — for independent sub-tasks like 'fetch from these 3 sources at once,' parallel dispatch is significantly faster. Sequential is required when later steps depend on earlier ones.

title: AI Slop
tldr: Low-effort, mass-produced AI output that floods the public web with mediocre or misleading content optimised for clicks rather than usefulness.
plain_english: AI slop is the generative-content version of spam: blogs, recipes, product listings, and images churned out by language models with little human oversight, then dumped onto the internet to chase ad revenue or search rankings. It's everywhere now — bland how-to articles that miss the point, fake book covers on retail sites, generic Linkedin posts. The problem is volume: slop crowds out real expertise and trains the next generation of models on its own mistakes.
how_it_works: A pipeline takes a topic list (often scraped from search-trend tools), runs each topic through an LLM with a prompt like 'write a 1000-word article on X,' optionally generates a stock image, and publishes to a CMS or marketplace. No fact-checking, no expert review, no original research. Identifying slop relies on signals: generic phrasing ('In today's fast-paced world...'), pattern-match openings, factual errors, suspiciously consistent structure across many pieces from one source, and disclosed metadata gaps.
why_it_matters: Slop pollutes search results, training data, and product catalogs. As models scrape the web for pretraining, their successors increasingly learn from prior slop, a feedback loop sometimes called model collapse. Consumers waste time on useless content; legitimate creators lose reach. For builders shipping AI features, recognising the slop pattern is how you avoid building it yourself.
example: A site posts 30 'best gadgets of 2026' articles per day, each generated from a product feed, each padded with the same vague intro and the same affiliate links. The articles rank because they target long-tail keywords, but they include hallucinated specs and recommend products that don't exist. Search traffic crashes a few months later when Google demotes the domain.
pitfalls: Treating slop as a content strategy: short-term traffic spikes precede long-term domain penalties as search engines learn to detect AI-only content.
Training on your own outputs: if your fine-tuning data includes prior model generations without quality filters, you compound error and lose distribution coverage (model collapse).
Confusing slop with all AI writing: high-quality LLM-assisted writing exists; the marker is human editing, fact verification, and a real point of view, not the toolchain.
Ignoring downstream harm: slop in medical, legal, or financial niches misleads vulnerable readers; even if it's profitable, the liability exposure is real.
when_use: There is no good 'when' — the term is a label for content patterns to avoid. Use the framing internally to evaluate your own pipelines: would this output qualify as slop if a critic reviewed it?
when_avoid: Avoid the slop pattern entirely. Build content where a domain expert is in the loop, a clear style guide constrains the model, and outputs are spot-checked before publishing.

faq.q: Is all AI-generated content slop?
faq.a: No. The label applies to content produced without expert review, original research, or genuine intent to inform — usually at scale and for ad revenue. Well-edited AI-assisted writing is not slop.

faq.q: How does Google handle AI slop?
faq.a: Google's spam policies treat unhelpful, low-effort content the same regardless of authorship. Sites identified as slop farms see ranking demotions, which is why pure-LLM content strategies tend to fail within months.

faq.q: What is model collapse and how does it relate?
faq.a: Model collapse is the degradation that happens when models train on outputs from earlier models. As more slop enters the public web, more of it gets scraped into next-gen pretraining sets, narrowing the diversity of generated text.

faq.q: How can I detect AI slop programmatically?
faq.a: Combine signals: perplexity scores, repeated phrasing patterns, factual checks against authoritative sources, and metadata anomalies (rapid publish cadence, generic author bios). No single signal is reliable; classifiers chain several.

title: Assisted Generation
tldr: Inference technique where a small draft model proposes tokens that a larger target model verifies in parallel, cutting wall-clock latency.
plain_english: Generating tokens one at a time is slow because the big model has to run end-to-end for every token. Assisted generation has a smaller, faster model write a draft of the next several tokens, then the big model verifies all of them in a single forward pass. Most drafts are right, the big model accepts them, and the system effectively skips ahead. When a draft token is wrong, only the prefix up to the mistake is kept and the system continues from there.
how_it_works: Pick a draft model that uses the same vocabulary as the target. At each step, the draft model autoregressively generates k candidate tokens. The target model runs a single batched forward pass over the k+1 prefixes (original + each draft step) and produces logits for each position. Compare draft probabilities to target probabilities token by token; accept tokens until the first rejection. After rejection, sample from the corrected distribution and continue. Acceptance rates depend on draft-target similarity — same family or distilled drafts work best.
why_it_matters: Latency is the dominant cost of LLM serving and the dominant complaint from users. Assisted generation routinely delivers 2-3× speedups on common workloads with no change in output distribution (in lossless variants). Frameworks like Hugging Face's `assistant_model` argument and vLLM's speculative decoding implement it. This is one of the few inference optimisations that improves user-perceived latency without sacrificing quality.
example: A chatbot serves Llama-3-70B as the target with Llama-3-8B as the draft. For each user turn, the 8B drafts 4 tokens at a time. On code-heavy outputs the acceptance rate is ~85%, so each forward pass produces ~3.4 accepted tokens instead of 1, giving roughly 3× speedup with identical sampled output.
pitfalls: Vocabulary mismatch: draft and target must share the same tokenizer; otherwise alignment breaks and acceptance collapses.
Weak draft model: if the draft is far worse than the target, acceptance rates fall below 50% and the verification overhead outweighs the gains.
Long-tail traffic: acceptance varies by domain — code completion has high acceptance, creative writing has lower; benchmark on your own traffic before committing.
Memory pressure: running both models means double the weights resident; budget VRAM accordingly or use shared-weight variants like Medusa heads.
when_use: Use whenever you serve a large target model and have a smaller compatible draft. Particularly effective for code completion, structured outputs, and high-token-count chat where draft accuracy is high.
when_avoid: Avoid when the draft model is unavailable or radically smaller than the target (acceptance too low), when memory is tight, or when latency-per-token is already at the network/RPC floor.

faq.q: Is assisted generation lossy?
faq.a: Standard speculative decoding is mathematically lossless — output distributions match running the target alone. Some variants (like draft-only sampling at high acceptance thresholds) trade tiny quality drops for more speed.

faq.q: How is this different from speculative decoding?
faq.a: Assisted generation is the user-facing name in libraries like transformers; speculative decoding is the underlying algorithm. The terms are often used interchangeably.

faq.q: What draft size should I use?
faq.a: Start with k=4 to k=8 tokens. Acceptance rates drop as k grows, but each successful round saves more time. Tune k against your traffic until the speedup peaks.

faq.q: Does assisted generation work with quantised models?
faq.a: Yes. The draft and target can be at different quantisation levels. A heavily quantised draft (e.g. INT4) plus an FP16 target is a common high-throughput configuration.

title: Attribution & Citation
tldr: Linking each claim in an LLM answer back to the source document that justifies it, so users and auditors can verify.
plain_english: When a RAG system says 'the policy renews on December 1,' attribution means it tells you which document and which paragraph it got that from. A citation is the actual marker rendered in the answer — a number, a footnote, a source link. Together they turn an opaque generation into a checkable answer, which is essential for legal, medical, and high-stakes use.
how_it_works: After retrieval supplies passages, the prompt instructs the model to cite passage IDs inline (e.g. '[1]') for any claim it makes. Post-generation, a parser maps citation markers back to passages and renders them as links. More robust systems run a faithfulness check: split the answer into claims, score each claim's similarity to the cited passage, and either flag or strip uncited claims. Some pipelines force structured output (each sentence carries a passage_id field) instead of inline markers to avoid parser errors.
why_it_matters: Citations turn an LLM answer into something auditable. Users can verify before acting; compliance teams can prove what evidence the system used; engineers can debug why a wrong answer was generated by reading the cited passage. Without citations, hallucinations are indistinguishable from grounded answers and trust erodes fast.
example: A legal-research assistant answers 'What's the deadline to appeal?' with 'You have 30 days [§4.2]. The clock starts the day the order is served [§4.3].' The citations link to the relevant statute sections so the lawyer can confirm in one click. Without them, the lawyer would have to re-read the whole policy to trust the answer.
pitfalls: Cited but unsupported: the model writes a plausible citation marker even when no passage actually supports the claim; always run a faithfulness check.
Over-citation: citing every sentence with the same source clutters output; collapse adjacent citations or cite once per paragraph.
Stale citation IDs: if you renumber passages between turns, old citations point to the wrong source; use stable doc:section IDs instead of array indices.
Hidden context: citations only cover what's explicitly stated; the model may correctly infer something the cited passage hints at but doesn't directly say, which auditors will flag.
when_use: Use whenever answer accuracy must be verifiable: legal, medical, finance, customer support escalations, regulatory reports, and any user-facing output where a wrong answer carries cost.
when_avoid: Avoid for casual chitchat, brainstorming, or creative writing where there is no source to cite. Also avoid when the underlying corpus is so noisy that citations would just propagate misinformation.

faq.q: What's the difference between attribution and citation?
faq.a: Attribution is the underlying mapping (claim X comes from source Y). Citation is the user-visible rendering of that mapping (the [1] in the text). You need both: attribution for correctness, citation for usability.

faq.q: Why does the model sometimes cite the wrong passage?
faq.a: LLMs treat citation markers as text tokens, not as structured links. The model can write [1] when [2] would be correct because the surface text doesn't enforce alignment. Structured output formats and post-generation faithfulness checks fix this.

faq.q: Should I cite by document, section, or sentence?
faq.a: Section-level is usually the sweet spot — granular enough to verify, coarse enough to stay readable. Sentence-level helps in regulated domains; document-level is too vague for high-stakes use.

faq.q: How do I evaluate citation quality?
faq.a: Run a faithfulness scorer on a labelled set: given each generated claim and its cited passage, does the passage entail the claim? Track precision (citations are correct) and recall (claims have citations) separately.

title: Bias Evaluation
tldr: Measuring whether a model produces systematically different outputs for inputs that differ only by protected attributes like gender, race, or age.
plain_english: Bias evaluation asks: if I keep everything else the same and only change the demographic detail, does the model treat the inputs differently? Maybe it writes warmer letters for one name versus another, or recommends harsher penalties for the same offense when the defendant's background changes. Bias evals quantify these gaps with structured test sets so teams can track whether new model versions, prompt tweaks, or fine-tunes are improving or regressing.
how_it_works: Build a paired test set: for each scenario, generate variants that differ only in protected attributes (e.g. swap names indicative of different ethnicities or pronouns). Run the model on each variant and compute output-level metrics: sentiment difference, recommendation rate, harmful-content rate, refusal rate. Aggregate per attribute group and compare. Common benchmarks include BBQ, BOLD, StereoSet, and Winogender; production teams supplement with task-specific paired prompts. Statistical tests (e.g. equality-of-proportions) confirm gaps aren't noise.
why_it_matters: Models trained on web-scale data inherit web-scale stereotypes. Without bias evals, those stereotypes silently flow into hiring tools, content moderation, and customer interactions. Regulatory frameworks (EU AI Act, NYC AEDT) increasingly require documented bias testing for high-stakes systems. Even outside regulation, a bias scandal can sink a product launch.
example: A team building a resume screener runs a paired test: identical resumes with names swapped between common Black and white American first names. The model recommends interviews 18% more often for the white-name variant. The team retrains with debiased prompts, reruns the eval, and the gap drops to 3% — still measurable, now disclosed in the model card.
pitfalls: Single-axis testing: measuring only gender bias misses intersectional gaps (Black women may be impacted differently than Black men or white women); test combinations.
Benchmark gaming: training to pass a specific eval (BBQ, etc.) without addressing root causes inflates scores while real-world bias persists.
False reassurance from low-stakes prompts: a model that's unbiased on 'write a story' may be deeply biased when asked 'should we hire this candidate?' — eval on task-relevant prompts.
Static test sets: societal definitions of bias evolve and adversaries find new gaps; refresh evals quarterly and after every fine-tune.
when_use: Use before any production deployment where outputs influence people: hiring, lending, content moderation, generative imagery, healthcare triage, and education tools.
when_avoid: There is no safe 'when avoid' for high-stakes deployments. For purely internal tools or research code, bias evals can be deferred but should still be on the roadmap before user-facing launch.

faq.q: Is bias eval the same as fairness testing?
faq.a: Bias eval is one component of fairness testing. Fairness testing also covers calibration, equal opportunity, and demographic parity — statistical properties of model decisions across groups.

faq.q: Which benchmark should I start with?
faq.a: BBQ for question answering, StereoSet for general associations, BOLD for open generation. Start with whichever matches your task most closely, then add task-specific paired prompts.

faq.q: Does fine-tuning fix bias?
faq.a: Sometimes, partially. RLHF and DPO with carefully curated preference data reduce many surface biases but rarely eliminate them, and can introduce new ones. Always re-run evals after every fine-tune.

faq.q: How often should I run bias evals?
faq.a: At minimum: every model version change, every prompt-template change, and quarterly on the static benchmarks. Production systems also run a sampled eval on live traffic.

title: Canary Deployment (LLM)
tldr: Routing a small percentage of live traffic to a new model version while keeping most users on the stable version, to catch regressions before full rollout.
plain_english: When you ship a new model, you don't know how it'll behave on real traffic — only on offline evals. A canary release sends maybe 1% of users to the new model, watches the metrics for a few hours or days, and only ramps up if quality, latency, and cost all hold. The old model serves the other 99%, so a regression hurts a tiny slice of users instead of everyone.
how_it_works: A traffic router (load balancer, feature flag, or LLM gateway) splits requests between the current model and the candidate based on a percentage rule. Telemetry collects per-route metrics: latency p95, error rate, user thumbs-up/down, output-length distribution, hallucination flags from a judge model. After a fixed bake period, the system either ramps the canary toward 100% or rolls back. Sticky routing (same user always hits the same version) prevents users from seeing inconsistent behaviour mid-conversation.
why_it_matters: Offline evals miss things live traffic surfaces: prompt patterns you didn't test, edge-case tools, latency under load, cost spikes from unexpected long outputs. A canary catches regressions in hours instead of days, with a contained blast radius. For LLM systems specifically, where output quality can be subjective, canaries also let you collect real user feedback before full rollout.
example: A coding assistant ships a new Llama-3.1 fine-tune. The team routes 2% of completion requests to it, keeps 98% on the previous version, and monitors completion-acceptance rate (did the user keep the suggestion?). After 24 hours the canary's acceptance is 1.2 pp higher than the baseline; the team ramps to 25%, then 50%, then 100% over the next three days.
pitfalls: Sample size: 1% of low-volume traffic may not produce statistically meaningful signal in a reasonable bake window; pre-compute the minimum traffic needed for your effect-size detection.
Sticky routing missed: if the same user lands on different versions across turns, the experience is jarring and your metrics get noisy.
Slow regressions: some failure modes (gradual hallucination drift, cost creep) only show up after days; bake longer than 'feels safe' for high-stakes deployments.
Forgetting rollback paths: a canary is only safe if the rollback button works under load; rehearse rollback as part of every release.
when_use: Use for any production model swap: new base model, new fine-tune, new prompt template, new RAG corpus, new tool registry. The bigger the change, the smaller the initial canary slice should be.
when_avoid: Avoid only when you genuinely cannot split traffic (single-tenant on-prem with one user) or when offline evals are the contractual gate (regulated rollouts). Even then, dogfood with internal users first.

faq.q: What's the difference between canary and shadow deployment?
faq.a: Canary serves real traffic to the candidate model and the user sees its outputs. Shadow runs the candidate in parallel but discards its outputs — users only see the stable model. Shadow is safer; canary is faster to learn from.

faq.q: How do I pick the canary percentage?
faq.a: Inverse to risk: high-stakes (legal, medical) starts at 0.1-1%; everyday chat at 5-10%. The target is enough traffic to detect a meaningful regression in your bake window.

faq.q: What metrics matter most?
faq.a: User-side: thumbs-down rate, retry rate, session length. System-side: latency p95, error rate, cost per request, output length. Quality-side: judge-model approvals on a sampled subset.

faq.q: Do I need a feature-flag system to canary?
faq.a: Not strictly. A simple load-balancer rule that hashes user IDs and routes by hash works. Feature flags add convenience and per-user overrides, which help when stakeholders want to test the canary on demand.

After a fixed bake period, the system either ramps the canary toward 100% or rolls back. Sticky routing (same user always hits the same version) prevents users from seeing inconsistent behaviour mid-conversation.

title: Cost Attribution
tldr: Tagging every LLM call with metadata so total spend can be split by feature, customer, team, or environment for chargeback and optimisation.
plain_english: When your monthly AI bill is $40k, you need to know where it went. Cost attribution attaches labels to each model call — which user triggered it, which feature called it, which model was used — so you can roll up the bill afterwards and answer 'how much did chat cost vs. summarisation' or 'is one customer driving 30% of spend?' Without this you can't price your product, can't catch runaway features, and can't justify infra optimisation work.
how_it_works: A wrapper around your LLM client adds tags to every request: user_id, feature_id, environment, model, and any custom dimension. The wrapper logs token counts and pricing into a metrics store keyed by these tags. Reports query the store by dimension to produce per-feature dashboards. Some teams use OpenTelemetry-GenAI semantics; others build custom Prometheus + Grafana stacks; LLM gateways (Helicone, Langfuse, Phoenix) ship attribution out of the box.
why_it_matters: Without attribution, you can't price-cap a single noisy customer, can't decide whether the new feature is profitable, and can't tell whether prompt-shortening work paid off. With it, you get the same observability finance teams expect for cloud costs — by team, project, environment — applied to LLM spend, which is now the fastest-growing infra line for many SaaS products.
example: An analytics SaaS adds attribution tags to every OpenAI call: customer_id, plan_tier, feature ('insights', 'chart-explainer', 'export-summary'). At month-end, finance discovers that the chart-explainer feature accounts for 62% of spend but is bundled in the free tier. The team adds a daily quota for free users, and spend on that feature drops 70% within a week.
pitfalls: Tag drift: tags must be stable across services; if team A uses 'feat=chat' and team B uses 'feature=chat', rollups silently miscount.
PII in tags: avoid putting raw user emails or full prompts in cost dimensions; hash IDs and keep the dimension cardinality bounded.
Async work: background jobs and retries can lose context unless the tags propagate through job queues; bake attribution into the queue payload.
Pricing changes: token costs change when the model does; store the per-call cost at write time, not just the token counts, so historical reports stay accurate.
when_use: Use as soon as monthly LLM spend matters to anyone (finance, product, infra) — typically when the bill crosses $1-5k/month or when multiple features share a model.
when_avoid: Avoid only for true prototypes where you'll throw the code away. Otherwise, retrofitting attribution into a mature codebase is painful; bake it in early.

faq.q: Should I tag at the SDK or gateway level?
faq.a: Both work. Gateway-level (Langfuse, Helicone, Portkey) captures attribution centrally and survives client-library changes. SDK-level is simpler if you only have one client.

faq.q: How granular should tags be?
faq.a: Coarse enough to keep cardinality bounded (a few hundred unique dimension values), fine enough to answer the questions you'll actually ask. Customer + feature + environment is a strong baseline.

faq.q: Does cost attribution slow down requests?
faq.a: Properly implemented (async write to a metrics buffer), the overhead is sub-millisecond. Poorly implemented (synchronous database writes per request), it can dominate latency.

faq.q: Can I attribute cost to a specific prompt template?
faq.a: Yes — add a prompt_id tag tied to your prompt registry. This is how teams identify which template change caused a cost spike or regression.

title: Critique Loop
tldr: Pattern where one LLM call generates an answer and a second call critiques it, using the critique to refine until quality bar is met or budget runs out.
plain_english: Your first answer is rarely your best one. A critique loop bakes that into the system: model produces draft, critic model points out flaws, model revises, critic checks again, repeat. The critic asks targeted questions — 'are the citations real?', 'is the math correct?', 'are there contradictions?' — and the rewriter addresses each. After a couple of iterations the answer is usually noticeably better, at the cost of two to four extra calls.
how_it_works: The loop has three roles: generator, critic, and stopping rule. The generator produces a candidate answer. The critic, given the same task plus the candidate, returns a structured list of issues (or 'no issues'). The generator regenerates with the critique appended to context. Stopping conditions: critic returns empty issues, max iterations reached, or budget exhausted. Some implementations score each critique to weight which issues are most important; others use the same model in both roles with role-shifting prompts. Constitutional AI is a famous instance.
why_it_matters: On reasoning-heavy and factual tasks, critique loops measurably improve accuracy without retraining. They turn a fixed-budget single-shot prompt into a quality-tunable system: spend more compute when the user wants more accuracy, spend less when latency matters. They also separate generation from evaluation, which is more debuggable than one mega-prompt that tries to do both.
example: A code-review assistant uses a critique loop: the generator drafts review comments, the critic checks each comment for ('is this an actual bug?', 'is the suggested fix correct syntax?', 'is the tone constructive?'). Iteration 2 drops two false-positive comments and rewrites one. The PR author sees a tighter, more accurate review.
pitfalls: Critic too lenient: if the critic always says 'looks good', the loop adds cost without improving quality; specify concrete failure modes in the critic prompt.
Infinite revision: without a hard cap, models will keep finding nits forever; cap iterations and require diminishing-returns stopping.
Same-model bias: when generator and critic share weights, blind spots are correlated; using a different model family as critic catches more issues.
Cost blow-up: each iteration is at least two LLM calls; track per-task iterations and alert when distributions skew long.
when_use: Use when output quality matters more than latency: legal/medical drafts, code generation, financial summaries, or any task where one wrong fact carries large downstream cost.
when_avoid: Avoid for chat-style or low-stakes outputs where users want a fast first answer, or when the marginal quality gain from a second pass doesn't justify doubling the cost.

faq.q: How is a critique loop different from self-reflection?
faq.a: Self-reflection has the model evaluate its own answer in the same call. A critique loop separates generation and critique into distinct calls, often with different prompts or models. The latter is more rigorous but costlier.

faq.q: Can the critic be smaller than the generator?
faq.a: Sometimes — for narrow checks (does this contain PII? is the JSON valid?), a small classifier or rules engine works. For nuanced critiques (is the argument coherent?) the critic typically needs to be at least as capable as the generator.

faq.q: How many iterations are enough?
faq.a: Empirically, 2-3 iterations capture most of the gain; beyond that, returns diminish quickly. Set a hard cap of 4 and stop early when the critic returns no issues.

faq.q: Does this help with hallucinations?
faq.a: Partially. A critic that fact-checks against retrieved documents catches many hallucinations; a critic that only reads the answer can't detect them. Pair the loop with retrieval for best results.

title: Data Flywheel
tldr: Compounding loop where production usage generates data that improves the model, which attracts more usage, which generates more data.
plain_english: A data flywheel is a self-reinforcing loop. Users interact with your AI feature, those interactions become labelled training data (thumbs-up, edits, escalations), the next model version gets better, which attracts more users, who generate more data, and so on. The competitive advantage is durable because it depends on usage volume — not just on having a good model. Companies with a working flywheel pull ahead of competitors who only buy access to the same base models.
how_it_works: Three loops run in parallel. (1) Capture: log every interaction with structured signals — accept/reject, edit distance, follow-up question, completion time. (2) Curate: filter the log into clean training pairs — promote signal-rich examples, drop noise, deduplicate, scrub PII. (3) Train: fine-tune the model (or its retrieval index, or its prompts) on the curated data and ship the new version. Each turn of the wheel typically takes one to four weeks. The faster you can close the loop, the steeper your improvement curve.
why_it_matters: Base models are commodities — anyone can call the same API. The differentiator is what you do with proprietary usage data. A working flywheel converts every user session into a small training-set contribution, so a six-month-old product with a million sessions has signals competitors can't replicate. This is also the only proven defence against new entrants once base-model prices fall.
example: A documentation-search startup logs which retrieved chunks users clicked on and which they ignored. Weekly, they fine-tune their retriever on click-through pairs. After three months, their domain-specific retrieval beats off-the-shelf embeddings by 14 NDCG points; competitors who use raw OpenAI embeddings can't catch up without their click data.
pitfalls: Selection bias: positive interactions over-represent users who already trust the system; curate with stratified sampling to avoid drifting toward only confident answers.
Privacy leaks: raw user prompts can contain PII or trade secrets; build a scrubbing pipeline before any data enters training.
Reward hacking: models trained on thumbs-up signals learn to write outputs that earn thumbs-up, which isn't always the same as outputs that are correct.
Stale loops: if a release takes 3 months, the data feeding it is 3 months old and the loop barely turns; aim for ≤2-week iteration cadence.
when_use: Use whenever you ship a user-facing AI feature that runs at non-trivial scale (≥1k interactions/day). The earlier you instrument capture and curate, the sooner the wheel starts turning.
when_avoid: Avoid the framing for prototypes (no users yet, no flywheel possible) or when legal/regulatory constraints prevent retraining on user data — in healthcare or EU consumer products, consent flows must come first.

faq.q: Do I need a fine-tunable model to have a flywheel?
faq.a: No. The flywheel can also improve retrieval (better index), prompts (auto-prompt-eval), or routing (which model handles which intent). Fine-tuning is the highest-leverage form but not the only one.

faq.q: How much data is enough to retrain?
faq.a: Depends on technique. Adapter fine-tuning meaningfully shifts behaviour with hundreds of examples; full fine-tunes need tens of thousands. Retrieval index updates can incorporate any volume.

faq.q: How do I avoid the flywheel feeding the model its own mistakes?
faq.a: Filter data on quality signals (user edits as positive, thumbs-down as negative), maintain a held-out human-curated eval set, and run model-collapse checks (does the new model lose tail capabilities present in the previous one?).

faq.q: Is more data always better?
faq.a: No. After a point, quantity stops mattering and quality starts. Curated 10k examples often beat raw 1M examples, especially for instruction-tuning and preference data.

title: Embedding Drift
tldr: When the embedding distribution of new documents or queries diverges from the indexed corpus, retrieval quality silently degrades.
plain_english: Your RAG system was tuned on last year's docs. The vocabulary shifts, new product names appear, the embedding model gets updated, or query phrasing trends change — and suddenly relevant documents stop showing up at the top of results. The embeddings themselves still work, but the world has moved and the gap between query embeddings and indexed embeddings widens. That gap is embedding drift.
how_it_works: Drift comes from three sources. (1) Corpus drift: new docs use language the index doesn't reflect. (2) Query drift: users start asking about topics absent from the index. (3) Model drift: you upgrade the embedding model and existing vectors are no longer in the same space. Detection compares centroids and cluster shapes between time windows: rising mean cosine distance between same-cluster items, falling recall on a held-out eval set, or KL divergence over topic distributions. Mitigation: re-embed the corpus with the new model, refresh the index on a cadence, and run shadow evals after every embedding change.
why_it_matters: Most RAG quality regressions in production come from drift, not bugs. The system 'works' — no errors, latency is fine — but answers feel staler, citations look less relevant, and user satisfaction quietly drops. Without explicit drift monitoring, teams chase phantom prompt issues for weeks before realising the index is the problem.
example: A support assistant indexed product docs from 2024. In 2026 the company renames a key feature and ships new modules. Users ask about the new names; the system returns 2024 chunks that don't mention them. Recall on a fresh held-out eval drops from 78% to 62%. The team kicks off a re-embed of the latest docs and recall recovers.
pitfalls: Treating drift as an embedding-only problem: chunking strategy, metadata filters, and reranker prompts also drift; audit the whole pipeline.
Ignoring partial re-embeds: when you upgrade the embedding model, mixing old and new vectors silently breaks similarity search; re-embed everything.
No drift baseline: without a held-out eval set captured at launch, you can't quantify how much the system has slipped over time.
Drift fixes that miss the cause: re-indexing fixes corpus drift but not query drift; you may also need synonym expansion, query rewriting, or fresher training data.
when_use: Use the framing as a monitoring practice for any RAG or semantic-search system in production for more than a few months, especially in domains with frequent vocabulary changes (product, news, legal).
when_avoid: Drift monitoring is overkill for static corpora that never change (archived legal codes from 1980) or for systems still in early development where the index changes daily anyway.

faq.q: How often should I check for embedding drift?
faq.a: Weekly for active corpora, monthly for stable ones. Tie checks to your deployment cadence — every model or chunking change should trigger a drift baseline.

faq.q: What's the simplest drift metric?
faq.a: Recall@k on a fixed eval set. If the same set of (query, expected_doc) pairs returns fewer correct documents this week than last, something drifted.

faq.q: Do I need to re-embed when I rotate keys?
faq.a: Only when you change the embedding model itself. Key rotation that keeps the model and version stable doesn't affect vectors.

faq.q: Is drift the same as catastrophic forgetting?
faq.a: No. Catastrophic forgetting describes a model losing capabilities after fine-tuning. Embedding drift is a distribution gap between data and index, with no model retraining required.

title: Embodied AI
tldr: AI systems that act through a physical or simulated body — robots, drones, vehicles — using sensors, actuators, and learned policies to interact with the world.
plain_english: Embodied AI is what you get when a model isn't just answering questions but actually moving something — an arm, a wheeled robot, a drone — and seeing the consequences. The model takes camera and sensor input, decides the next action (turn left, grasp, open the gripper), and the world changes. That feedback loop is what makes embodiment hard: every mistake has physical cost, and the model has to learn from rare, expensive trials.
how_it_works: An embodied system has three layers. Perception turns sensors (RGB, depth, lidar, IMU) into a state representation. Policy maps state to action — increasingly a transformer or vision-language-action (VLA) model that conditions on goal and context. Control executes the action through actuators with safety limits. Training combines imitation learning from demonstrations, reinforcement learning in simulation (Isaac, MuJoCo), and small amounts of real-world fine-tuning. Recent VLA models like RT-2, OpenVLA, and π0 unify all three layers as a single transformer that emits action tokens.
why_it_matters: Embodied AI is the path from chatbot to general-purpose helper — the Hollywood vision of AI that does laundry, drives, performs surgery. The technical bottleneck has shifted from algorithms to data: there isn't enough robot interaction data on the internet, so progress depends on simulation, teleoperation collection, and clever transfer from internet-scale image-language data. The field is moving fast as VLAs and shared datasets like Open X-Embodiment grow.
example: A warehouse robot uses an RGB camera and a VLA model that takes 'pick up the blue cup and put it on the tray' as natural language. The model emits action tokens at 30 Hz, the controller turns those into joint commands, and a safety layer caps acceleration. Failures are recorded for the next round of fine-tuning.
pitfalls: Sim-to-real gap: a policy that works perfectly in simulation often fails on real hardware due to physics, sensor noise, and lighting differences; use domain randomisation aggressively.
Reward shaping abuse: hand-crafted rewards in RL training can produce policies that exploit shortcuts (knocking objects over to satisfy a 'distance' reward); prefer human demonstrations.
Data scarcity: robot interaction data is orders of magnitude smaller than text data; pretraining on video and action-free trajectories partially closes the gap.
Safety regressions: a fine-tune that improves task success can quietly introduce unsafe behaviours like over-fast motion; always rerun safety evals after every weight change.
when_use: Use the framing for any system where an LLM or vision model controls physical actuators: robotic manipulation, autonomous mobility, drone navigation, or even mouse-and-keyboard agents acting on a desktop GUI.
when_avoid: Avoid for purely textual or screen-based agents that don't sense physical state; the embodied framing adds complexity (sensors, control loops) that pure software agents don't need.

faq.q: How is embodied AI different from a regular AI agent?
faq.a: A software agent acts through APIs and tools; an embodied agent acts through actuators in physical or simulated space. The distinction matters because physical actions have continuous, irreversible consequences that pure-software agents don't face.

faq.q: What is a vision-language-action (VLA) model?
faq.a: A transformer trained to take image + language goal as input and emit motor-action tokens as output. It unifies perception, planning, and control in one model. RT-2, OpenVLA, and π0 are widely cited examples.

faq.q: Can I run embodied AI experiments without a robot?
faq.a: Yes, in simulation. Isaac Sim, MuJoCo, and Habitat let you train and evaluate policies without hardware. The catch is the sim-to-real gap; policies that work in sim still need real-world fine-tuning.

faq.q: Why is data the bottleneck?
faq.a: Internet-scale text contains trillions of tokens. Robot interaction data is at the millions-of-trajectories scale at best, often dataset-specific. Shared datasets (Open X-Embodiment) and large-scale teleoperation efforts are the main remedies.

title: Evaluation Set
tldr: A curated, fixed collection of inputs and expected outputs used to measure model quality, separate from the training set, for repeatable comparison across versions.
plain_english: An eval set is your benchmark. You build a list of representative inputs — questions, prompts, screenshots — and either label correct outputs or define a scoring rule. Every time you change the model, prompt, or pipeline, you run the eval set again and compare. The set itself never changes (or changes rarely, in versions), so the numbers mean something. Without a stable eval set, every release feels subjective; with one, regressions show up in the diff.
how_it_works: Source examples from real traffic, customer escalations, and adversarial probes (cases you want to fail loudly on). Label the expected behaviour: exact answer, structured output schema, or rubric for graders. Store as JSON, YAML, or a parquet file under version control. Run the eval as a CI step: pipeline produces outputs, scorer compares against expected, results aggregate to per-category scores. Track changes over time in a dashboard; alert on regressions.
why_it_matters: Without a fixed eval set you can't tell whether a model swap helped or hurt. With one, every change is measurable, every regression is reproducible, and arguments about quality become evidence-based instead of vibes-based. Eval sets are also the only way to gate releases: if the score on the eval set drops below a bar, the deploy is blocked.
example: A code-completion product maintains a 500-prompt eval set covering Python, JavaScript, SQL, and Bash. Each prompt has 1-3 acceptable completions. A scorer marks model output as match if it equals any acceptable completion. The team gates releases on ≥85% match; the latest fine-tune scores 87%, ships; the next prompt change drops to 81%, blocks.
pitfalls: Eval set leaks into training: if you scrape the same data both for fine-tuning and for eval, scores become meaningless; keep a hard partition.
Static set goes stale: eval examples reflect the world from when you built them; refresh quarterly or when traffic patterns shift.
One-number reporting: a single aggregate score hides regressions in important sub-categories; always report per-tag breakdown.
Overfitting to the eval: optimising prompts solely against the eval set produces good scores and bad real-world behaviour; cross-validate with held-out data and human review.
when_use: Use from day one of any AI feature. Even 30 hand-picked examples beat no eval set; you can grow it as you learn what matters.
when_avoid: Avoid only for one-off prototypes you'll never iterate on. Anything that ships to users — even internally — needs at least a minimal eval set.

faq.q: How big should my eval set be?
faq.a: Start with 30-100 examples that cover the main intents and edge cases. Grow to several hundred as you discover failure modes. Beyond ~1000, returns diminish unless you stratify by sub-category.

faq.q: Should the eval set match production traffic distribution?
faq.a: Mostly yes, but add adversarial examples to over-represent the failure modes you most care about catching. A pure-distribution sample under-covers tail risks.

faq.q: How do I score open-ended outputs?
faq.a: Use a rubric (a list of yes/no checks) and either a human grader or an LLM-as-judge. For factual answers, exact-match against a list of acceptable strings works.

faq.q: What's the difference between an eval set and a holdout set?
faq.a: A holdout set is a generic term for any data not used in training. An eval set is a specific, curated benchmark used to measure quality during development. The eval set is usually a subset of holdout data with labels.

title: Faithfulness
tldr: Property of a RAG answer that every claim it makes is supported by the retrieved passages, with no fabricated or unsupported additions.
plain_english: Faithfulness is the discipline test for RAG. Even with great retrieval, the model can wander off-script and add plausible-sounding details that aren't in the cited passages. A faithful answer says only what the passages support, no more. Measuring faithfulness — how many of the answer's claims are actually grounded — is the difference between an assistant you can trust and one that hallucinates between citations.
how_it_works: Decompose the model's answer into atomic claims (one fact each). For each claim, check whether the retrieved passages entail it: a small LLM-as-judge or NLI classifier scores entailment per (claim, passage) pair. Aggregate to a faithfulness score: percentage of claims with at least one entailing passage. RAGAS, TruLens, and Phoenix ship faithfulness scorers; or you can roll your own with a sentence-level entailment model. Track faithfulness alongside answer relevance; high relevance with low faithfulness is a hallucinator that happens to be on-topic.
why_it_matters: Hallucination kills RAG trust. Citations alone don't fix it — the model can cite a passage and still claim something the passage doesn't say. Faithfulness scoring catches these unsupported claims so you can add training signal, prompt fixes, or refusal logic to address them. In regulated domains (legal, medical) faithfulness is a release gate.
example: A medical-info assistant retrieves a passage about acetaminophen dosage. Its answer says '500-1000mg every 4-6 hours' — supported, faithful. It also adds 'avoid if you have peanut allergies' — the passage says nothing about peanut allergies. Faithfulness scorer flags the second claim as unsupported; the team adds a refusal-on-uncited-claim rule.
pitfalls: Atomic-claim splitting errors: bad sentence segmentation merges supported and unsupported claims into one, hiding the unsupported parts.
Judge model bias: an LLM judge that's lenient about entailment inflates scores; calibrate against a human-labelled subset.
Confusing faithfulness with correctness: an answer can be faithful (matches the passage) but the passage itself can be wrong; faithfulness only measures grounding.
Ignoring negative claims: if the model says 'X is safe' but the passage says 'X has side effects,' a naive entailment check misses the contradiction; check for entailment AND non-contradiction.
when_use: Use as a continuous quality metric for any production RAG, especially in regulated or high-stakes domains. Use it as a release gate: drop in faithfulness blocks a deploy.
when_avoid: Avoid for free-form creative tasks where the model is supposed to extrapolate beyond sources. Faithfulness scoring penalises legitimate creativity and gives misleading numbers in those settings.

faq.q: How is faithfulness different from grounding?
faq.a: Grounding describes the property of being anchored to evidence. Faithfulness is the measurable degree of grounding — the metric you compute on a specific answer.

faq.q: What's a good faithfulness score?
faq.a: Depends on domain. Casual chat tolerates ~80%; medical/legal/financial assistants should target ≥95% with refusal on uncited claims.

faq.q: Can I improve faithfulness without retraining?
faq.a: Yes. Better prompts that explicitly forbid unsupported claims, retrieval quality fixes, and post-generation faithfulness checks with refusal-on-fail all help significantly.

faq.q: Is faithfulness the same as factual correctness?
faq.a: No. An answer can be faithful to a wrong passage and therefore factually wrong. Faithfulness measures grounding; factual correctness requires the source itself to be correct.

short_definition: Property of a RAG answer that every claim it makes is supported by the retrieved passages, with no fabricated or unsupported additions.
full_explanation: Decompose the model's answer into atomic claims (one fact each). For each claim, check whether the retrieved passages entail it: a small LLM-as-judge or NLI classifier scores entailment per (claim, passage) pair. Aggregate to a faithfulness score: percentage of claims with at least one entailing passage. RAGAS, TruLens, and Phoenix ship faithfulness scorers; or you can roll your own with a sentence-level entailment model. Track faithfulness alongside answer relevance; high relevance with low faithfulness is a hallucinator that happens to be on-topic.

title: Function Schema
tldr: Structured description of a tool's inputs, outputs, and behaviour that an LLM uses to decide when and how to call it.
plain_english: Before an LLM can call a function, it has to know what the function expects and returns. The function schema is that spec: name, description, parameter types, required vs. optional fields, and ideally examples. The model reads the schema, decides which function fits the user's request, and emits a call with arguments that match the schema. If the schema is vague or wrong, the model picks the wrong function or fills wrong arguments.
how_it_works: Schemas are usually JSON Schema or a JSON-Schema-shaped object the LLM provider expects. Each function has: name (snake_case), description (one sentence about purpose and when to use), parameters (a JSON-Schema object with property descriptions, types, enums, and required list). At inference, the schema is included in the system prompt or via a structured tools field. The model returns a tool_call object with the function name and arguments object. A runtime parser validates against the schema and rejects malformed calls before execution.
why_it_matters: Function schemas are the contract between LLM and code. Good schemas reduce hallucinated calls (model invents a function), wrong-argument errors, and ambiguity over which tool fits. The OpenAI and Anthropic tool-use APIs both rely on schema quality; switching between providers usually means converting between near-identical schema dialects. As tool counts grow into the dozens, schema discipline becomes a real engineering concern.
example: A weather agent exposes get_weather(location: string, units: 'celsius'|'fahrenheit'='celsius'). Description: 'Return current weather for a city. Use when the user asks about temperature, humidity, or conditions.' The model receives this schema, sees user input 'how warm is it in Tokyo?', emits {name: 'get_weather', arguments: {location: 'Tokyo'}}.
pitfalls: Vague descriptions: 'Get information' tells the model nothing; spell out exactly when to call and not call the function.
Loose types: parameters typed as 'string' when the function accepts only specific values lead to invalid calls; use enums.
Schema bloat: registries with 50+ tools blow up context size and degrade selection accuracy; group tools and use a router.

Provider-specific quirks: OpenAI accepts JSON Schema with caveats; Anthropic uses input_schema with slight differences; Google has its own dialect; abstract over them or you'll fork your tool registry.
when_use: Use whenever you connect an LLM to executable code or external APIs. Even single-tool setups benefit from explicit schemas — the model still needs to know argument shape.
when_avoid: Avoid manual schema authoring when you can derive schemas from typed source code (Python type hints, TypeScript interfaces); generate them programmatically to stay in sync.

faq.q: Are JSON Schema and function schema the same?
faq.a: Function schema usually IS JSON Schema, with provider-specific extensions for things like strict mode or tool descriptions. The terms are often used interchangeably in LLM contexts.

faq.q: Can I generate schemas from my code?
faq.a: Yes. Libraries like Instructor, Pydantic AI, and Outlines convert typed Python classes into schemas automatically. This is the recommended approach to keep code and schema in sync.

faq.q: How many tools can I expose at once?
faq.a: Modern LLMs handle 5-20 well; beyond 30 selection accuracy and prompt cost suffer. For larger registries, group tools and use a router or MCP-style discovery.

faq.q: Why does the model sometimes invent function names?
faq.a: Because the prompt encodes function names as text tokens; the model can hallucinate plausible names even when not in the schema. Strict-mode validation at the SDK level catches and rejects these calls.

title: Grounding
tldr: Anchoring an LLM's answer to specific external evidence — documents, database rows, sensor data — so the response reflects facts rather than the model's priors.
plain_english: An ungrounded model answers from memory. Asked about your company's refund policy, it'll write something plausible but wrong. A grounded model is shown the actual policy text first, then asked, so the answer reflects what the document says. Grounding is the umbrella concept under RAG: retrieve the right context, force the model to use it, and check that the output stays anchored to that context.
how_it_works: Three steps. (1) Identify the right evidence for the query — usually retrieval over a vector or keyword index. (2) Inject the evidence into the prompt with clear delimiters and instructions ('Answer using only the passages below'). (3) Verify the answer is anchored: faithfulness checks, citation parsing, refusal when no passage supports the claim. Production systems combine these with confidence thresholds; if the top retrieved passages have low similarity to the query, the system either escalates or returns 'I don't know.'
why_it_matters: Grounding is what makes LLMs useful for domain-specific work. Without it, every answer is a coin flip on whether the model's pretraining covered your domain. With it, you can deploy LLMs over private data, specialised knowledge, or recent events the model has never seen, with citations and faithfulness as safety nets. The whole RAG industry exists to make grounding work at scale.
example: A customer-service assistant answers 'Can I cancel my plan?' Without grounding: 'Most services allow cancellation within 30 days.' With grounding (after retrieving the company's actual policy): 'You can cancel anytime within 14 days of purchase for a full refund [policy §3.2]. After 14 days, the cancellation takes effect at the end of your billing cycle.'
pitfalls: Retrieval misses: grounding only works if the right passages are retrieved; weak retrieval makes the model fall back on priors.
Conflicting evidence: when retrieved passages contradict each other, the model may pick one, blend them, or hallucinate; surface conflicts explicitly.
Over-grounding: forcing the model to quote even for common-knowledge questions makes answers stilted; calibrate when grounding is needed.
Stale evidence: grounded answers are only as fresh as the indexed corpus; couple grounding with index-update cadence and version tracking.
when_use: Use whenever the answer should reflect specific, verifiable evidence: company knowledge, regulated content, recent events, technical references, or any factual question where a wrong answer carries cost.
when_avoid: Avoid for creative or open-ended tasks where the model's priors are exactly what you want — story writing, brainstorming, hypotheticals. Grounding constraints would only get in the way.

faq.q: Is grounding the same as RAG?
faq.a: RAG is a specific architecture for grounding: retrieve documents, then generate. Grounding is the broader concept — any technique that anchors output to evidence, including tool calls, structured database lookups, and live API responses.

faq.q: How do I tell if my model's grounded?
faq.a: Run a faithfulness score on a sample of outputs. Faithful answers cite passages and stay within them; ungrounded answers contain claims the passages don't support.

faq.q: Can I ground without retrieval?
faq.a: Yes — small corpora or specific contexts can be passed in the prompt directly without a retrieval step. This works for short documents but doesn't scale to large corpora.

faq.q: What if the retrieval returns nothing useful?
faq.a: Build refusal logic. If similarity scores are below a threshold or the model can't construct a faithful answer, return 'I don't have information about that' rather than letting the model guess.

title: GRPO
tldr: Group Relative Policy Optimization — RL fine-tuning algorithm that compares multiple sampled outputs against each other to estimate advantage without a value model.
plain_english: Standard RLHF needs a value-network to estimate how good each token is. GRPO drops that. Instead, for each prompt it samples a group of completions, scores them all with the reward model, and uses the group's relative scores as the learning signal. Higher than the group average means push the policy toward those tokens; lower means pull away. Removing the value model cuts memory and compute roughly in half and stabilises training. DeepSeek used GRPO to train R1 and several reasoning-focused models adopted it after.
how_it_works: For each prompt, sample N completions (typically 4-16) from the current policy. Score each with a reward model or rule-based scorer. Compute the group mean and standard deviation, then derive an advantage per completion as (reward - group_mean) / group_std. Use these advantages with a clipped policy-gradient objective (similar to PPO's clip term) to update the policy. KL divergence against a reference model keeps the policy from drifting too far. No value network is needed because group statistics provide variance reduction.
why_it_matters: GRPO removed a major engineering cost (value-network training) and improved sample efficiency for reasoning fine-tunes. It became the algorithm of choice in 2025-2026 for math, code, and reasoning training at frontier labs. Open-source frameworks (TRL, OpenRLHF, verl) added GRPO support fast, making it the default starting point for groups training reasoning models without a frontier-lab budget.
example: A team trains a math-reasoning model. For each problem, they sample 8 completions, run a unit-test verifier as the reward (1 if all tests pass, 0 otherwise), and apply GRPO. After 5k steps the model's pass rate on a held-out math benchmark rises 14 points without ever training a value model.
pitfalls: All-zero or all-one groups: when every sample passes or fails, group std is zero and the gradient explodes; clip or skip degenerate groups.
Reward hacking: if the verifier has loopholes (e.g. tests that pass with empty stubs), the policy learns to exploit them; harden verifiers before training.
KL coefficient tuning: too low and the model drifts away from the base; too high and it can't learn anything new; sweep this hyperparameter early.
Sample inefficiency at scale: GRPO requires N completions per prompt, multiplying inference cost; budget compute accordingly.
when_use: Use for RL fine-tuning of reasoning models, code-generation models, or any task with a rule-based or fast learned verifier. Particularly attractive when GPU memory is the bottleneck.
when_avoid: Avoid when reward signal is noisy or sparse (small groups won't differentiate), when you have a high-quality value model already trained, or when DPO/SFT alone meet quality goals at lower complexity.

faq.q: How is GRPO different from PPO?
faq.a: PPO uses a separate value model to estimate advantages; GRPO uses group statistics from multiple samples per prompt. Both clip the policy update similarly, but GRPO removes a major training cost.

faq.q: How is GRPO different from DPO?
faq.a: DPO trains directly on preference pairs without on-policy sampling. GRPO is online RL — it samples completions during training and uses a reward signal. GRPO can use rule-based rewards; DPO requires preference data.

faq.q: What group size should I use?
faq.a: 8 is a common starting point. 4 is faster but noisier; 16+ is more stable but slows iteration. Tune against your compute budget and reward-signal noise.

faq.q: Does GRPO replace SFT?
faq.a: No — GRPO usually runs after a supervised fine-tune to refine behaviour. Starting GRPO from a base model with no SFT typically gives unstable training.

title: Guidance (Library)
tldr: Microsoft library that interleaves Python control flow with constrained LLM generation, letting you specify exact output structure inline with the prompt.
plain_english: Guidance is what you reach for when you want a model to follow a specific output template strictly. Instead of post-processing JSON or regretting a malformed response, you write the prompt with placeholders for what the model should generate where, and Guidance forces the model to fill them in. It uses regex, grammars, or structured templates to constrain decoding so impossible outputs literally cannot happen.
how_it_works: You write a guidance program that mixes static text and gen() / select() calls. At runtime Guidance applies token-level masks during decoding: for select() the only allowed tokens are those completing one of the choices; for gen() with a regex/grammar, only tokens compatible with the regex are sampled. Loops and conditionals control which calls fire next. Guidance supports both local models (Llama, Mistral) and remote APIs that allow prefix continuation, but constraints work best with local models where token-level control is exposed.
why_it_matters: When you need machine-readable output that downstream code parses without retries, constrained generation libraries like Guidance, Outlines, and LMQL turn 'almost-always-correct' into 'always-correct.' This makes LLM outputs viable inputs to typed code, databases, and APIs without heavy validation/retry logic.
example: A medical-form filler uses Guidance to produce structured output: program defines `{ 'symptom': gen(regex='[a-zA-Z ]+'), 'severity': select(['mild','moderate','severe']), 'duration_days': gen(regex='[0-9]+') }`. Whatever the model says, the output strictly matches that schema, no validation retries needed.
pitfalls: Constraint conflicts with model preference: forcing the model into outputs it strongly dislikes can produce gibberish; if a regex is too restrictive the constrained tokens get vanishingly low probability.
API support varies: not every provider exposes prefix continuation; cloud APIs like OpenAI work with subset features, local models give full control.
Performance hit: token-level masking adds modest overhead; large grammars slow generation more than small regex.
Lock-in to library style: prompts written with Guidance look different from raw prompts; portability across libraries (Outlines, Instructor) requires rewriting.
when_use: Use when output must conform exactly to a schema (database rows, function arguments, code snippets) and you want to skip retry-on-malformed loops.
when_avoid: Avoid for free-form creative outputs (writing, dialogue) where constraints would degrade quality. Also avoid when your provider doesn't support fine-grained constraints — you'll end up emulating Guidance at the SDK level.

faq.q: How is Guidance different from Outlines?
faq.a: Both implement constrained generation. Guidance has stronger control-flow primitives and was designed around interleaving Python and prompt; Outlines focuses on regex/JSON-Schema constraints with simpler syntax. Many teams pick by ergonomic preference.

faq.q: Does Guidance work with OpenAI?
faq.a: Partially. OpenAI supports JSON-mode and structured outputs, which cover many use cases, but token-level grammars require local model access. Guidance abstracts the differences but loses fine-grained control on cloud APIs.

faq.q: Is Guidance still actively maintained?
faq.a: Yes — Microsoft continues to maintain it as of 2026. The space moves fast; check release dates and consider alternatives like Outlines or Instructor for a current comparison.

faq.q: Can I use Guidance for tool calling?
faq.a: Yes, by constraining function-call output to your tool schema. In practice teams pair Guidance with Pydantic-derived schemas for type safety end-to-end.

title: Holdout Set
tldr: Data deliberately excluded from training so it can be used for unbiased measurement of how the model performs on examples it has never seen.
plain_english: If you train on every example you have, you can't tell whether your model is actually learning the task or just memorising. A holdout set is the data you set aside and never touch during training. After training, you measure performance on the holdout. If accuracy is high on training data and low on the holdout, you've overfit. If both are similar, the model generalises. The holdout is also where you compare different model versions fairly.
how_it_works: Split your labeled dataset before any training. Conventional ratios are 80% train / 10% validation / 10% test; the test split is the strict holdout. Use the validation split during development to tune hyperparameters and pick model variants. Reserve the test split for the final, one-shot measurement before release. Stratify the split so class distributions match across train/val/test. Lock the holdout in version control with a hash so anyone can verify the split hasn't drifted.
why_it_matters: Holdout discipline is the difference between published numbers that hold up in production and ones that look good then collapse. Repeated tuning against the same holdout silently leaks information into the model and inflates scores. Locked, untouched holdouts are how labs report trustworthy benchmarks and how teams catch regressions at release time.
example: A team labels 10,000 customer-support tickets with intent categories. They split 8,000 train / 1,000 val / 1,000 test, hash the file lists, and lock the test set. During development they iterate against val. Before release they run the model on test once: 92% accuracy. They publish that number with confidence because the test set was unseen.
pitfalls: Test-set contamination: any tuning, prompt-engineering, or feature engineering that uses test scores leaks into the model and inflates reported numbers.
Distribution mismatch: a holdout drawn from old data won't reflect current production traffic; refresh holdouts as the world changes.
Tiny holdouts: 100 examples can't differentiate a 1-2 point quality change reliably; size the holdout to your effect-size detection needs.
Reusing the same holdout for years: even without explicit tuning, design choices accumulate that implicitly target known failure modes; rotate holdouts periodically.
when_use: Use whenever you train, fine-tune, or even prompt-engineer a model. Even a 50-example holdout gives more discipline than no holdout at all.
when_avoid: There is no good 'when avoid' — even one-off prototypes benefit from quick holdout checks. The only place a holdout is irrelevant is when there's no labeled data at all.

faq.q: What's the difference between holdout, validation, and test sets?
faq.a: Validation is for development-time tuning. Test is the strict, untouched holdout for final measurement. 'Holdout set' is sometimes used to mean either, but the test split is the canonical one.

faq.q: How big should the holdout be?
faq.a: 10-20% of total data is a common default. The minimum is whatever's needed to detect meaningful effect sizes — a few hundred examples for binary tasks, more for multi-class.

faq.q: Can I use the same holdout across model versions?
faq.a: Yes, that's the point — comparing scores on the same locked holdout makes versions comparable. Just be aware that prolonged use can lead to implicit overfitting; rotate periodically.

faq.q: What if my data is too small to split?
faq.a: Use cross-validation: rotate which fold serves as the holdout across multiple training runs. Gives unbiased estimates with small datasets at the cost of more compute.

title: Inference Batching
tldr: Combining multiple LLM requests into a single GPU forward pass to amortise compute and increase throughput per dollar.
plain_english: GPUs work most efficiently when they have lots of math to do at once. Sending one request at a time leaves most of the GPU idle. Batching groups several user requests into a single forward pass — same model weights, multiple inputs — so the GPU stays busy. Throughput goes up dramatically; per-request latency may rise slightly because the request has to wait for the batch to fill. The trade-off is the central tuning knob in LLM serving.
how_it_works: An inference server queues incoming requests. Static batching waits until N requests arrive (or a timeout) and processes them together. Dynamic batching adds requests as they arrive up to a size cap. Continuous batching, the modern default, lets new requests join an in-flight batch on every step, freeing slots as completed sequences leave. vLLM, TGI, and TensorRT-LLM implement continuous batching with paged attention for memory efficiency. Throughput scales nearly linearly with batch size until memory or attention compute saturates.
why_it_matters: Batching is the single biggest lever for cost-per-token. A well-batched server can run 10-50× more requests per GPU than a naive serial server. Without it, hosting your own LLM is rarely cheaper than calling an API; with it, self-hosting becomes economically competitive. For SaaS pricing, batching efficiency directly determines gross margin on AI features.
example: A SaaS team self-hosts Llama-3-8B for chat. With sequential serving they get 12 req/s on one A100. After enabling vLLM with continuous batching, throughput jumps to 230 req/s while p95 latency stays under 1.5s. The team retires three of four GPUs and saves ~$3k/month.
pitfalls: Stragglers: a single very long sequence holds up the batch; cap max output tokens and use streaming to mitigate user-perceived lag.
Memory blow-up: larger batches mean larger KV cache; without paged attention, memory fragmentation kills throughput at high batch sizes.
Latency-throughput trade: tuning for max throughput often hurts p95 latency; pick a batching policy that matches your SLA.
Variable input lengths: padding short inputs to the longest in a batch wastes compute; bucket by length or use ragged batching.
when_use: Use whenever you self-host LLMs at any non-trivial scale (≥10 req/min). Modern inference servers (vLLM, TGI, SGLang) batch by default; always enable it.
when_avoid: Avoid only for true one-at-a-time interactive demos where latency dominates and you're sized for one user. Even small teams should batch in production.

faq.q: What's the difference between static and continuous batching?
faq.a: Static batching processes a fixed-size batch as a unit. Continuous batching lets new requests join an in-flight batch step by step, dramatically improving GPU utilisation for chat-style workloads.

faq.q: How does batching affect latency?
faq.a: Per-token latency is similar to single-request, but there's additional queue wait time. Time-to-first-token may rise slightly; total throughput rises significantly.

faq.q: Does batching require special hardware?
faq.a: No — any GPU benefits. The bigger and more parallel the GPU (A100, H100), the more batching pays off; on consumer GPUs the benefits are still real but smaller.

faq.q: Can I batch across users?
faq.a: Yes, that's the point. Privacy considerations are about prompt content, not batch slots; a batch is a runtime detail invisible to users.
