title: Semantic Chunking
tldr: Splitting documents into chunks based on meaning shifts rather than fixed character or token counts, so each chunk stays coherent.
plain_english: Naive chunking splits at character N or token N. That tears sentences apart and chops topics in half. Semantic chunking watches the meaning and splits where the topic shifts: between sections, after a transition sentence, when an embedding similarity drops below a threshold. The chunks that result match the document's actual structure, which makes retrieval and downstream LLM use noticeably better.
how_it_works: Several strategies. (1) Embedding-based: embed each sentence, compute similarity between adjacent sentences, split where similarity drops below a threshold. (2) Topic-segmentation: use a small classifier or LLM to label topic shifts. (3) Structure-aware: respect markdown headings, HTML sections, code blocks. (4) Recursive: chunk by largest natural boundary first (sections), then smaller (paragraphs), then sentences, until size targets met. LangChain, LlamaIndex, and Unstructured ship semantic-chunking utilities; some teams roll bespoke for their content shape.
why_it_matters: Chunking is the most under-appreciated lever in RAG. A document chunked badly means every retrieval result is partially wrong. Semantic chunking can lift downstream answer quality by 10-20% with no other changes — same model, same retriever, just better chunks. For long-form content (books, manuals, legal documents), the gap between naive and semantic chunking is huge.
example: A team chunks a 200-page user manual. Naive 1000-char chunks split a step-by-step tutorial mid-step. Semantic chunking splits at heading boundaries plus embedding-similarity drops, producing chunks that each cover one complete tutorial step. Question-answering recall@5 rises from 61% to 78%.
pitfalls: Threshold tuning: embedding-similarity-drop thresholds are content-specific; one threshold doesn't fit all corpora.
Too-small chunks: aggressive splitting fragments context the model needs; balance coherence with chunk count.
Too-large chunks: respecting boundaries can produce chunks that don't fit the LLM's context budget; cap with a max-size guard.
Overlap discipline: many chunkers add overlap between adjacent chunks; semantic chunkers should still overlap or downstream context can be lost at boundaries.
when_use: Use for any RAG over structured documents (manuals, books, legal text, technical docs). Also valuable when naive chunking is hurting answer quality on coherence-sensitive content.
when_avoid: Avoid for short documents that fit whole into context, for chat-style logs where chunks have no natural structure, or when the marginal quality gain doesn't justify the extra preprocessing cost.

faq.q: How big should semantic chunks be?
faq.a: Match the natural structure of your content. Tutorials per step, FAQ per Q&A, legal per clause. As a fallback, 500-1500 tokens per chunk is a common range.

faq.q: Embedding-based or LLM-based chunking?
faq.a: Embedding-based is fast and cheap; LLM-based is slower but understands content better. Production systems often combine both: structure-aware first (cheap), then LLM-based refinement on outliers.

faq.q: Should I overlap semantic chunks?
faq.a: Yes, modestly. Even structure-aware splits can lose context at boundaries; 10-20% overlap is a safe default.

faq.q: Does this work for code?
faq.a: Yes, with code-aware splitting. Chunk at function/class boundaries, not arbitrary character counts. Most modern code-RAG systems use AST-aware chunking.

title: Shadow Deployment (LLM)
tldr: Running a candidate model in parallel with the production model, comparing outputs offline, but only serving the production model's responses to users.
plain_english: A canary releases the new model to some users. Shadow deployment runs the new model on the same traffic but throws its outputs away — users always see the production model. The team gets to compare the two on real traffic without risk. The trade is that you can't measure user behaviour against the candidate (since they never see it), only output-level differences.
how_it_works: Each incoming request gets duplicated: production handles the user-visible response; the candidate model runs the same input asynchronously. Both outputs are logged with the request. Offline comparison evaluates differences: similarity score, judge-model preferences, latency, cost, faithfulness on RAG queries. Aggregated reports surface where the candidate differs and whether it's better or worse. Once the team is confident, they promote the candidate via a canary or a direct switch.
why_it_matters: Shadow is the safest way to evaluate a model on real traffic before risking it on users. Offline benchmarks miss real-world distribution; canaries have user impact if regressions exist. Shadow has neither problem — you get production-traffic signal with zero user risk. The cost is doubled inference compute on shadowed traffic and the engineering work of duplicate calls.
example: A search engine shadows a new embedding model: every query goes to both models, both result sets are logged, and offline analysis measures NDCG against click-through. After a week, the new model wins on 7 of 10 query categories; the team rolls it out via canary, no user-visible disruption.
pitfalls: Doubled cost: shadowing 100% of traffic doubles inference spend; sample to 10-20% if budget matters.
Comparison difficulty: subjective output quality is hard to compare automatically; combine judge-model scores with human spot-checks.
Side-effect risk: if the candidate makes external calls (writes data, sends emails), shadow-mode must mock those out to avoid duplicate side effects.
Latency contribution: even if shadow runs async, it consumes resources that might tighten capacity for production; isolate shadow infrastructure.
when_use: Use for major model swaps (different base, big fine-tune) where regressions would be costly. Especially valuable in regulated settings where user-impact testing is restricted.
when_avoid: Avoid for minor changes (prompt tweaks, version bumps) where canary is cheaper and simpler. Also avoid when comparison costs (judge models, human review) are prohibitive.

faq.q: Shadow vs canary — which first?
faq.a: Shadow first to prove the candidate doesn't catastrophically regress; canary second to measure user-visible impact. Shadow is risk-free signal; canary is signal with bounded risk.

faq.q: How long should shadow run?
faq.a: Long enough to capture traffic variation: 1-2 weeks for typical workloads, longer if traffic patterns are weekly. Tie length to confidence interval on your comparison metric.

faq.q: How do I compare outputs automatically?
faq.a: Use a judge model (LLM-as-judge) to score same-input outputs side-by-side. Track judge agreement with human spot-checks to validate the auto-comparison.

faq.q: Does shadow work for streaming?
faq.a: Yes, but storage gets bulky. Save the full token stream for both models; use sampled streaming if that's expensive.

full_explanation: Each incoming request gets duplicated: production handles the user-visible response; the candidate model runs the same input asynchronously. Both outputs are logged with the request. Offline comparison evaluates differences: similarity score, judge-model preferences, latency, cost, faithfulness on RAG queries. Aggregated reports surface where the candidate differs and whether it's better or worse. Once the team is confident, they promote the candidate via a canary or a direct switch.

title: Shortcut Learning
tldr: When a model achieves high training accuracy by exploiting spurious correlations rather than learning the intended task — fragile to distribution shift.
plain_english: Models are lazy: they take any shortcut that works on training data. If 'positive sentiment' correlates with the word 'great' in your training set, the model might learn 'output positive' instead of actual sentiment understanding. This works on data that looks like training data and falls apart on new distributions. Shortcut learning is why models pass benchmarks and fail in production.
how_it_works: Whenever there's a feature easier to learn than the true task and that feature predicts the label well in training data, the model preferentially learns the shortcut. Examples: image classifiers learning 'grass = cow' because cows are usually photographed in fields; medical NLP models learning 'mentions hospital_name = positive case' because that hospital had high case rates in the training period; question-answering models learning 'answer is always the longest noun phrase'. Detection: distribution-shift evaluation, counterfactual probes (remove the shortcut feature; performance should hold), and training-data analysis.
why_it_matters: Shortcut learning is the silent failure mode of every production AI system. Training accuracy looks great; deployment performance silently underperforms; nobody knows until a customer complaint reveals it. Defending against shortcuts requires deliberately diverse training data, counterfactual evaluation sets, and ongoing distribution monitoring — none of which are free.
example: A loan-approval classifier learns to use ZIP code as a strong predictor. On training data ZIP correlates with creditworthiness; the classifier exploits this. In deployment, the classifier amplifies geographic bias and fails fair-lending review. Diagnosis: counterfactual swap of ZIP shows the model's predictions change dramatically — clear shortcut signal.
pitfalls: Trusting in-distribution accuracy: high test scores on data drawn from the same source as training don't tell you whether the model uses real features or shortcuts.
Removing one shortcut, missing another: models find new shortcuts when old ones are blocked; counterfactual eval needs to cover many spurious features.
Dataset imbalance amplifying shortcuts: rare-class examples have weaker signal, so the shortcut feature carries more weight; balance carefully.
Fine-tuning making it worse: a shortcut-prone base model fine-tuned on biased data can lock in the shortcut more strongly than the base alone.
when_use: Use the framing during model development, eval design, and post-deployment monitoring. Any production ML system benefits from shortcut-aware evaluation.
when_avoid: There's no good 'when avoid' — shortcut awareness is a baseline practice. The only situation where you can ignore it is in throwaway prototypes that won't ship.

faq.q: How is shortcut learning different from overfitting?
faq.a: Overfitting is memorising training examples; shortcut learning is using a working-but-wrong feature. A shortcut-learning model can have low training error AND low test error (on similar data) and still fail in deployment.

faq.q: How do I detect shortcuts?
faq.a: Counterfactual evaluation: change the suspected shortcut feature while keeping the label-relevant features constant; if model predictions change, the model relies on the shortcut.

faq.q: Do bigger models avoid shortcuts?
faq.a: Sometimes — capacity to learn the real task helps — but big models also have more capacity to find subtle shortcuts. Scale alone doesn't solve it.

faq.q: Is data augmentation a fix?
faq.a: Partially. Augmentations that break the shortcut (e.g. paraphrasing, image perturbations) help. Targeted counterfactual training (consciously varying suspected shortcuts) is more effective.

title: Sub-Agent
tldr: Specialised agent invoked by a parent agent to handle a focused sub-task, returning a result without taking permanent control.
plain_english: When an agent encounters a task it can't or shouldn't handle itself, it spawns a sub-agent: a specialised assistant focused on one thing — searching the web, generating code, drafting a section. The parent waits for the sub-agent to finish, takes the result, and continues. Unlike a handoff (control transfers permanently), a sub-agent is more like a function call — it returns and the parent resumes.
how_it_works: The parent agent has a registry of available sub-agents (research_agent, code_agent, writer_agent). When its plan calls for one of those skills, it invokes the sub-agent with a focused prompt: goal, relevant context, expected output schema. The sub-agent runs its own LLM loop with restricted tools, produces a result, returns to the parent. Parent integrates the result into its working state. Common implementations: LangGraph subgraphs, AutoGen group chats with a coordinator, function-call style 'delegate_to(agent_name, task)' tools.
why_it_matters: Sub-agents let you compose. A general-purpose agent that knows how to delegate beats one that has every tool itself: smaller prompts, focused tool sets, better debugging. Sub-agents are also how you reuse agent capabilities — the same research_agent can be called by multiple parents. As production agent systems grow, sub-agent patterns are how you keep complexity manageable.
example: A marketing-content agent receives 'write a blog post about X.' Internally it calls a research sub-agent for facts, a writer sub-agent for the draft, and an SEO sub-agent for optimisation. Each sub-agent returns to the parent, which assembles the final post. None of the sub-agents need to know about each other.
pitfalls: Cost compounding: each sub-agent is an LLM loop; a parent that spawns 5 sub-agents per turn 5×s the bill.
Context loss in delegation: sub-agents only see what the parent passes; under-passing causes bad results, over-passing leaks irrelevant context.
Latency: sub-agents run sequentially by default; parallelise where outputs are independent.
Recursion: sub-agents that invoke their own sub-agents can recurse without limit; cap depth explicitly.
when_use: Use when one agent's responsibilities cleanly split into specialist skills, when you want focused tool sets per role, or when capabilities should be reusable across multiple parents.
when_avoid: Avoid for simple linear workflows (a pipeline is cheaper), latency-tight paths, or when the sub-agent boundary is unclear (don't force decomposition for its own sake).

faq.q: Sub-agent or tool call?
faq.a: Tool calls run code; sub-agents run their own LLM loop. If the work is mechanical (DB query, math), tool. If it requires judgment or multi-step reasoning, sub-agent.

faq.q: Should sub-agents share state with the parent?
faq.a: Minimally. Pass what the sub-agent needs; let it return what the parent needs. Shared mutable state across agents is hard to debug.

faq.q: How is this different from a handoff?
faq.a: A handoff transfers control permanently — the new agent owns the conversation. A sub-agent does one piece of work and returns control to the parent. Sub-agents are function-call-like; handoffs are role transitions.

faq.q: Can sub-agents themselves spawn sub-agents?
faq.a: Yes, with depth caps. Deep nesting gets expensive and hard to debug; production systems usually cap at 2-3 levels.

title: Swarm Agent
tldr: Pattern where many simple agents work in parallel on parts of a problem, sharing partial results through a common workspace.
plain_english: A swarm is the opposite of a hierarchical agent system. Instead of one orchestrator coordinating specialists, many lightweight agents each work on small pieces of the same problem. They publish progress to a shared workspace; other agents read what's there and decide what to work on next. The whole thing converges (or doesn't) through emergent coordination rather than top-down planning. Inspired by ant colonies and similar biological swarms.
how_it_works: Spawn N worker agents, each with a small system prompt and tool set. Provide a shared blackboard (a key-value store or document) where partial results, claims, and pending tasks live. Each agent runs a loop: read blackboard, decide if there's work it can contribute, do that work, post results. Swarms can use voting, leader election, or simple race semantics. OpenAI's Swarm framework popularised lightweight handoff-style swarms; multi-agent debate is a constrained swarm; some research swarms have 50-100 agents collaborating on long-form writing or simulation.
why_it_matters: Swarms can outperform hierarchical agents on tasks where parallelism + diversity matter more than coordination: brainstorming, exploration, simulation, distributed search. They're also more resilient — losing one agent doesn't break the system. The downside is that emergent behaviour is hard to predict and debug; swarms work best when the task tolerates noise and outliers.
example: A code-search swarm scans a large codebase for security issues. 20 agents each take a different module, run static checks plus LLM review, and post findings to a shared list. Duplicates are merged; high-confidence findings are escalated to a senior reviewer agent. The swarm covers more code in parallel than a single agent could sequentially.
pitfalls: Coordination overhead: too many agents reading and writing the same blackboard creates contention; partition the workspace by sub-task.
Reduplication: without deduplication, multiple agents do the same work; assign claims or use locks.
Convergence: swarms can fail to converge on contentious decisions; pair with an arbitrator or voting rule.
Cost: many agents = many LLM calls; cap swarm size for budget.
when_use: Use for parallelisable exploration: code review, document summarisation, brainstorming, simulation, distributed search. Especially valuable when diversity of approach matters.
when_avoid: Avoid for sequential dependencies (one step gates the next), tight latency budgets, or any task where you need predictable, auditable behaviour.

faq.q: Is OpenAI Swarm the same as a swarm agent?
faq.a: OpenAI Swarm is a specific framework using lightweight handoff between agents; it's a thin layer rather than a full multi-agent platform. Swarm-agent the pattern is broader and predates OpenAI's library.

faq.q: How many agents work best?
faq.a: Depends on task. Pair-style swarms (2-3) are common for debate; brainstorming/exploration swarms can have dozens. Diminishing returns set in fast above 10-20 for most tasks.

faq.q: Do swarm agents need a shared LLM?
faq.a: No. Mixing model families increases diversity of thought and often improves outcomes, especially in debate and brainstorming.

faq.q: Can swarms handle disagreement?
faq.a: Several patterns: majority voting, weighted by confidence, or escalation to an arbitrator. Choose based on whether you want the median answer or want to surface conflicts.

title: Task Decomposition
tldr: Breaking a complex task into smaller sub-tasks that the model handles individually, then combining the results into a final answer.
plain_english: If you ask a model to do too much at once, it cuts corners on each part. Task decomposition splits the work: identify the sub-tasks, do each one separately, then combine. The model handles each sub-task with full attention; the combiner step assembles the pieces. This is the single most important reliability technique for multi-step problems — both for prompts and for agent systems.
how_it_works: Two flavours. Static decomposition: hand-write the sub-tasks ('first do X, then Y, then Z') and prompt the model through each in sequence. Dynamic decomposition: ask the model to identify the sub-tasks itself, then iterate. Each sub-task is its own prompt or tool call. Output of each goes into the next prompt's context. A final synthesis prompt assembles the answer. Plan-and-solve, plan-and-execute, agent loops, and pipeline architectures all rest on task decomposition.
why_it_matters: On any task with 3+ logical steps, decomposition consistently beats single-prompt attempts. The model has narrower context per step, less to track, fewer chances to hallucinate. Production systems apply decomposition at several layers: prompt-level (CoT, plan-and-solve), workflow-level (pipelines), and agent-level (sub-agents). Mastering decomposition is most of the skill of building reliable LLM systems.
example: A financial-analysis prompt asks 'compare Company A and Company B and recommend an investment.' Naive: model gives a vague paragraph. Decomposed: (1) gather Company A financials, (2) gather Company B financials, (3) compute key ratios, (4) compare to industry, (5) recommend with rationale. Each step is its own prompt or tool call; the final answer is grounded in the prior outputs.
pitfalls: Over-decomposition: 10-step prompts for 2-step problems waste tokens and time.
Under-decomposition: leaving compound steps causes the model to skip parts; if you can break it further and it helps, do.
Lost context: each sub-task only sees what you pass; under-passing makes the sub-task fail.
Combination errors: synthesis at the end mishandles edge cases (one sub-task failed); design the combiner to handle missing or low-confidence parts.
when_use: Use for any task with multiple distinct logical steps. The first thing to try when a single-prompt attempt produces messy or incomplete output is decomposition.
when_avoid: Avoid for genuinely simple tasks (one fact lookup, one classification) where decomposition adds overhead with no benefit.

faq.q: How do I know when to decompose?
faq.a: If a single prompt gives inconsistent or incomplete results, try decomposition. Also when steps require different tools or different model strengths (research vs. writing vs. critique).

faq.q: Static or dynamic decomposition?
faq.a: Static is faster and predictable; use when the workflow is known. Dynamic is flexible; use when input variability requires different sub-task graphs per request.

faq.q: Does decomposition always cost more?
faq.a: Usually yes — multiple LLM calls beat one. But the alternative is unreliable output, which costs more in retries, manual review, and user trust.

faq.q: How does this relate to chain-of-thought?
faq.a: CoT is decomposition expressed as inline reasoning steps. Plan-and-solve, plan-and-execute, and pipelines are decompositions expressed as separate calls. Both are forms of the same idea applied at different scales.

title: Tool-Result Caching
tldr: Storing the outputs of agent tool calls keyed by arguments so identical re-invocations skip the actual call and return the cached result.
plain_english: Agents call tools repeatedly — same web search, same database query, same API call. Tool-result caching saves the output keyed by (tool_name, arguments) so the next identical call returns instantly without hitting the underlying service. It's normal HTTP caching applied to agent tool invocations. Cuts cost, latency, and load on downstream APIs significantly when traffic patterns repeat.
how_it_works: A wrapper around tool calls computes a cache key (often hash of name + sorted-args). Before invoking the tool, it checks the cache; on hit, returns the cached value. On miss, calls the tool, stores result with TTL, returns. TTL is per-tool: 5 min for stock prices, 1 hour for product info, 24 hours for static lookups. Some caches add semantic invalidation (purge when underlying data changes), per-user partitioning (privacy), or cost-based admission (only cache expensive calls).
why_it_matters: Tool calls dominate agent latency and cost for many workloads. A research agent that calls a search API 10 times per task with overlapping queries can cache 5-7 of those calls and slash both wall-clock time and API spend. Tool-result caching is the highest-ROI infra change for agent systems with repeated traffic patterns.
example: A customer-support agent calls a 'lookup_order' tool that hits the order-management DB. Without caching, every conversation about the same order makes a DB roundtrip each turn. With caching keyed on order_id and a 5-min TTL, the same conversation hits the DB once and reuses the result for 5 minutes. DB load drops 80%; agent latency drops 200ms per turn.
pitfalls: Stale data: caching things that change (account balance, inventory) returns wrong answers; tune TTL per tool sensitivity.
Privacy mixing: caching results across users when results are user-specific leaks data; partition cache keys by user_id.
Argument normalisation: small differences in argument formatting (whitespace, ordering) cause cache misses; normalise before key generation.
Cache invalidation: when underlying data changes, the cache returns wrong answers until TTL expires; either short TTLs or explicit invalidation hooks.
when_use: Use for any tool whose calls show repeating patterns (same arguments many times) and whose results are stable for at least a few seconds. Especially valuable for expensive or rate-limited tools.
when_avoid: Avoid for tools that must be fresh on every call (real-time bidding, mutex acquisition), for tools whose arguments don't repeat (UUIDs, timestamps), or when cache complexity outweighs savings.

faq.q: Tool-result caching or semantic caching?
faq.a: Different layers. Tool-result caching keys on exact tool args. Semantic caching matches similar prompts at the LLM layer. Both can run together; they cover different traffic.

faq.q: What TTL is right?
faq.a: Per-tool. Static reference data: hours to days. User-account data: minutes. Real-time data: seconds or no caching. When unsure, start short and lengthen if hit rate justifies.

faq.q: Should the cache be local or distributed?
faq.a: Local in-memory caches (LRU, TTLCache) are fast and simple; distributed (Redis) supports multi-replica deployments. Most production agent systems use Redis or similar to share cache across replicas.

faq.q: Can I cache POST requests?
faq.a: Yes, but only when the request is idempotent (same args → same result). Use the request body as part of the key. Don't cache mutating calls.

title: Tool Router
tldr: Component that picks which subset of available tools to expose to the LLM based on the current query, keeping context size and selection accuracy high.
plain_english: When an agent has 50 tools, dumping all schemas in the prompt blows up tokens and hurts the model's ability to choose. A tool router sits in front: it looks at the user's query and picks 5-10 most-relevant tools, sending only those to the LLM. The LLM then chooses among them. Routers can be rule-based (regex on the query), embedding-based (similarity to tool descriptions), or LLM-based (a small model picks).
how_it_works: Index every tool with its description as an embedding (or as routing rules). At query time, embed the user query, find top-k closest tool descriptions, and inject only those tools into the LLM prompt. For LLM-based routers, a small fast model is given the tool catalogue and the query and outputs the relevant tool subset. Modern systems often combine: embeddings for first-pass filtering, LLM for tie-breaking. MCP (Model Context Protocol) implements router semantics across federated tool servers.
why_it_matters: Tool count is the silent killer of agent scalability. Performance degrades sharply once you exceed 10-20 tools in a single prompt: more tokens, worse selection accuracy, more hallucinated tool names. A router fixes this — agents with 100s of tools become tractable because each prompt only sees the relevant 5-10. This is what makes 'do anything' agent platforms feasible.
example: A coding assistant has 60 tools (git, fs, eslint, test runners, deploy, etc.). For 'why is the test failing?', the router selects {test_runner, git_diff, file_reader}. For 'deploy to staging', it selects {git_status, deploy_tool, env_check}. Each prompt sees fewer than 10 tools, selection accuracy is high, and the agent runs faster.
pitfalls: Wrong tool excluded: a query the router didn't anticipate may need a tool it didn't include; provide an 'expand' fallback the agent can call.
Routing latency: the router itself is a model call; cache decisions per common query patterns.
Description quality: routers depend on tool descriptions being meaningful; vague descriptions make routing unreliable.
Privacy: tool descriptions may reveal capabilities you don't want exposed; sanitise before embedding.
when_use: Use whenever your tool count exceeds ~15. For large platforms (MCP-style federations) the router is mandatory.
when_avoid: Avoid for small tool sets (≤10) where the routing overhead isn't justified. Also avoid when latency budget is too tight to absorb an extra hop.

faq.q: Embedding-based or LLM-based router?
faq.a: Embedding routers are cheap and fast but miss intent nuance. LLM routers handle nuance but cost more. Production systems often combine: embeddings for first-pass, LLM for refinement.

faq.q: How many tools should the router output?
faq.a: 5-10 is a sweet spot. Fewer makes the LLM's choice trivial; more crowds the prompt and reduces selection accuracy.

faq.q: Can the agent ask for more tools mid-task?
faq.a: Yes — provide an 'expand' meta-tool the LLM can call to request additional tools matching a description. This handles the rare case where routing missed something.

faq.q: Does MCP have routing built in?
faq.a: MCP supports tool discovery (list available tools per server) but routing is the client's responsibility. Most MCP-aware clients implement embedding-based or rule-based routing on top.

title: Truthfulness Evaluation
tldr: Measuring how often a model gives factually correct answers vs. confidently asserting falsehoods, especially on questions designed to elicit common misconceptions.
plain_english: Truthfulness eval asks: when the model is wrong, does it admit uncertainty or confidently state something false? Models trained on internet text absorb common misconceptions and conspiracy theories along with everything else. Truthfulness evals (TruthfulQA being the most cited) probe with adversarial questions where a confident wrong answer is the easy mistake — and score how often the model dodges it.
how_it_works: Benchmarks like TruthfulQA pick questions where a confident wrong answer matches popular myths or conspiracy claims (e.g. 'What happens if you eat watermelon seeds?'). Score by how often the model gives a truthful answer (often a flat refusal of the myth or an evidence-grounded answer). Newer evals add multi-turn probes, generation-time evaluation, and combine with judge-model scoring. Production teams add domain-specific truthfulness sets covering their content area.
why_it_matters: Confidently wrong is the worst failure mode of LLMs. A user can't tell hallucination from fact without checking, and confident wrongness erodes trust faster than uncertainty. Truthfulness evals catch this directly. They're now part of standard model-card evaluations and required reporting in some regulated settings.
example: A consumer health assistant runs a truthfulness eval covering nutrition, symptoms, and treatments. The eval has 500 questions where common misconceptions appear in user search data. The model scores 76% on standard questions, 41% on misconception-laden questions. The team adds RAG over verified medical sources and re-runs: 89% / 78%. Ship gate at 75% on misconception set passes.
pitfalls: Benchmark gaming: training on TruthfulQA-style data improves the score without genuinely improving truthfulness on novel myths.
False refusal vs. truth: a model that refuses everything scores high on truthfulness (no falsehoods) but is useless; combine with helpfulness scoring.
Definition of truth: 'truth' on contested or evolving questions is debatable; eval sets need calibration and review.
Domain gap: general truthfulness benchmarks don't predict performance in specialist domains; build domain-specific evals.
when_use: Use as part of pre-release evaluation for any user-facing chat or assistant. Especially important in domains (health, finance, legal) where confident wrong answers carry user harm.
when_avoid: There's no good 'when avoid' for user-facing systems. Internal tools for known-skilled users tolerate more, but truthfulness baselines remain useful.

faq.q: Is TruthfulQA still the standard benchmark?
faq.a: It's the most widely cited, but heavily memorised by frontier models. Use it as a baseline and supplement with newer or domain-specific evals.

faq.q: How is truthfulness different from faithfulness?
faq.a: Faithfulness measures whether an answer is supported by retrieved evidence — grounded in source. Truthfulness measures whether the answer is correct in the world. Faithful and untrue (cited a wrong source) is possible; truthful and unfaithful (right answer, no citation) is also possible.

faq.q: Does RAG fix truthfulness?
faq.a: It helps a lot when the source corpus is reliable. Without trustworthy sources, RAG just adds confident citations to wrong answers.

faq.q: How often should I run truthfulness evals?
faq.a: On every model swap, prompt template change, and quarterly on a static set. Production deployments also sample live traffic and run truthfulness checks via judge models.

schema_jsonld.description: Measuring how often a model gives factually correct answers vs. confidently asserting falsehoods, especially on questions designed to elicit common misconceptions.

short_definition: Measuring how often a model gives factually correct answers vs. confidently asserting falsehoods, especially on questions designed to elicit common misconceptions.
full_explanation: Benchmarks like TruthfulQA pick questions where a confident wrong answer matches popular myths or conspiracy claims (e.g. 'What happens if you eat watermelon seeds?'). Score by how often the model gives a truthful answer (often a flat refusal of the myth or an evidence-grounded answer). Newer evals add multi-turn probes, generation-time evaluation, and combine with judge-model scoring. Production teams add domain-specific truthfulness sets covering their content area.

title: World Model
tldr: Internal representation of how the environment changes in response to actions, used by agents to simulate consequences before committing to a real-world action.
plain_english: A world model is the agent's internal physics engine. Before doing something, it imagines what would happen — moves the chess piece in its head, predicts the next sentence, simulates the car's trajectory. Good world models let agents plan ahead instead of trial-and-error in the real world. They're the centrepiece of model-based RL and are increasingly present in foundation models that learn implicit causal structure during pretraining.
how_it_works: Three approaches. Classical model-based RL trains a small dynamics model (state, action) → next_state from interaction data, then plans by rolling out simulated trajectories. Latent world models (DreamerV3, World Models by Ha & Schmidhuber) learn a compact latent space and predict transitions there, decoupling perception from dynamics. Foundation-model approaches (V-JEPA, GAIA, Sora) train massive video or sensor models that implicitly capture physics through next-token or next-frame prediction. The agent uses the world model to plan, by sampling possible action sequences and scoring outcomes.
why_it_matters: World models are increasingly central to AI progress. They power model-based reinforcement learning, robotics planning, video generation, and embodied AI. The bet many labs are making is that a sufficiently good world model — one that captures physics, causality, and intuition about how things change — is a major step toward general-purpose agents. As compute and data scale, world models that started as toy research projects (DreamerV1) are becoming production systems (Tesla FSD's planner, Wayve's driving model, OpenAI Sora).
example: A robotic-manipulation system has a world model trained on thousands of hours of arm-and-object interaction. Before grasping, the agent simulates 100 candidate trajectories in the world model, scores them by predicted success, picks the best, and executes. Real-world success rate doubles compared to a model-free policy with the same training budget.
pitfalls: Compounding error: tiny per-step prediction errors stack up over long rollouts; cap horizon or correct with real observations periodically.
Distribution shift: world models trained on one regime fail on novel situations; combine with safety constraints in deployment.
Cost: large world models are expensive to train and to query; budget compute carefully.
Imagination ≠ reality: agents that over-trust their world model take risky actions that look fine in simulation; pair with fallback safety logic.
when_use: Use the framing for model-based RL, robotic planning, video and physics generation, or any system where the agent must predict consequences before acting in the real world.
when_avoid: Avoid for purely textual or static-prediction tasks where there's no environment to model. Also avoid when interaction data is too sparse to train a useful model.

faq.q: What is JEPA?
faq.a: Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture — a self-supervised approach (Yann LeCun's research direction) that trains world models to predict in latent space rather than pixel space, learning structure without reconstructing every detail.

faq.q: Are LLMs world models?
faq.a: Implicitly, yes — they predict next tokens and pretraining captures causal text patterns. They're not explicit world models in the RL sense but increasingly serve a similar role for symbolic and language-mediated tasks.

faq.q: How does Sora relate to world models?
faq.a: Sora and similar video-generation systems are de facto world models for the visual domain — they predict how scenes evolve. Whether they count as 'true' world models for planning is debated; they capture some causality but make many physical errors.

faq.q: Can I build a world model for a custom environment?
faq.a: Yes, with interaction data. Standard recipes (DreamerV3, MuZero) take a few hundred to a few thousand episodes to learn useful dynamics for many environments. Quality scales with data and compute.
